title: Spotting Financial Scams Targeting Older Adults
summary: Scams targeting older adults are common, often sophisticated, and worth understanding well before they show up.
category: Life Transitions III: Legal & Financial Shifts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Spotting Financial Scams Targeting Older Adults | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Scams targeting older adults are common, often sophisticated, and worth understanding well before they show up.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Urgency and secrecy are the two most common scam tactics — legitimate requests rarely demand both at once.
Common scams involve fake grandchildren, government impersonation, and romance schemes.
A "let me call you back" habit stops most scams cold.

paragraphs: Scammers routinely rely on urgency ("act now") and secrecy ("don't tell your family") — a combination that legitimate businesses, government agencies, or family members almost never actually need. Recognizing that pattern alone stops a large share of attempts.

Common scripts include a call claiming to be a grandchild in trouble needing money urgently, a supposed government agency threatening arrest over unpaid taxes, or a romantic relationship that eventually asks for money. Establishing a habit of "let me call you back" before acting on any financial request defeats nearly all of these.

nextSteps: Talk through common scam patterns with your parent directly, without alarming them.
Set up a family code word for verifying real emergencies.
Consider a bank alert for large or unusual withdrawals.
disclaimer: General awareness guidance — report suspected fraud to the FTC or local authorities.

sources.name: Protecting Older Adults from Fraud and Financial Exploitation
